title: Russia Acknowledges Oil Output Decline as Ukraine Refinery Strikes Bite
subhead: Deputy Prime Minister Novak confirmed that Russian crude production has fallen since the start of 2026 due to Ukrainian drone attacks on refineries — the first public admission from Moscow that the campaign is working. Meanwhile, OPEC+ prepares to raise July quotas despite Hormuz disruption, and war-risk insurance premiums have jumped 1,000% for tankers transiting the Persian Gulf.

sections.heading: Russia Admits Production Decline as Refinery Strikes Intensify
sections.body:
Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak publicly acknowledged on Thursday that Russia's oil production has declined since the beginning of 2026, citing unscheduled repairs at multiple refineries damaged by Ukrainian drone strikes. Speaking at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum, Novak said Russia is 'maximizing the use of export infrastructure' to compensate for lost domestic refining capacity.

The admission marks a shift from Moscow's previous stance. Novak told reporters that 'a number of refineries are under unscheduled repairs,' attributing the outages to the drone campaign. Russia has banned jet fuel exports through November 2026 and gasoline exports since April 1 as it struggles to maintain domestic fuel supplies.

According to Oil Price reporting, the production cuts represent the steepest decline in six years, with analysts estimating 300,000-400,000 barrels per day of refining capacity offline. Ukraine has targeted major facilities including the 300,000 bpd Yaroslavl refinery and export terminals on the Baltic and Black Seas.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first official Russian acknowledgment that Ukrainian drone strikes are successfully degrading refining capacity. The export bans signal Moscow expects disruptions to persist through year-end, with implications for global fuel markets and Russia's fiscal position.

sections.heading: War-Risk Premiums Surge 1,000% for Hormuz Transit
sections.body:
Insurance costs for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz have skyrocketed more than 1,000% since the Iran conflict began, pushing additional war-risk premiums for Very Large Crude Carriers to $2.5-7.5 million per voyage, according to S&P Global Energy data cited by the Financial Express. A typical VLCC valued at $100-150 million now faces costs that have fundamentally disrupted tanker economics.

Before the conflict, war-risk premiums ranged from 0.1% to 0.3% of vessel value. They have since jumped to 2.5-5%, and even after easing to 0.8-1.5% by late March, remain well above pre-conflict levels. Medium Range product tankers face premiums of $80,000-120,000 for a seven-day transit.

The report notes that at a 50% traffic recovery rate, clearing the vessel backlog in the Gulf would take roughly two weeks. At only 10% traffic levels, clearance could extend to eight to nine weeks. Intermittent ceasefires have created a 'stop-start' pattern undermining market confidence.
sections.whyItMatters: Even if a formal peace agreement emerges, shipping companies and insurers are expected to maintain a cautious approach. The insurance premium spike has created a structural cost increase that will persist beyond any ceasefire, affecting global crude flows and freight rates.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Poised to Raise July Quotas Despite Hormuz Disruption
sections.body:
OPEC+ is likely to approve another output increase for July when the alliance meets, according to Reuters — the third quota hike since the Hormuz closure began. The increase remains largely theoretical while Gulf exports remain constrained by the Iranian conflict, but signals the group's intention to bring back supply when conditions normalize.

The UAE left OPEC on May 1 after nearly 60 years, with presidential advisers citing a desire to 'pump more as the end of the oil era looms' — a reference to both the production constraints and the accelerating energy transition. The UAE exit weakens the cartel's collective bargaining power but analysts expect the remaining members to stay coordinated.

Reuters reports that the production increase will remain 'largely on paper' as long as the Strait of Hormuz stays disrupted. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser previously warned that Hormuz disruption could push oil market recovery into 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is trying to signal supply-readiness while dealing with the reality that Gulf producers cannot easily move oil to market. The UAE exit removes roughly 3.2 million bpd of production capacity from OPEC's coordination framework, though Abu Dhabi continues bilateral cooperation with Riyadh.

sections.heading: Power of Siberia 2 Pipeline Gets Fresh Momentum from Iran Crisis
sections.body:
The Iran war has injected new urgency into negotiations over the Power of Siberia 2 gas pipeline between Russia and China, Bloomberg and Strait Times report. The proposed 4,000+ km conduit would carry 50 billion cubic meters annually — nearly equivalent to the now-dormant Nord Stream 1 — from Russia's Arctic Yamal fields to northeastern China via Mongolia.

On June 20, Presidents Putin and Xi are expected to discuss the project in Beijing. Gazprom CEO Alexey Miller has called it 'the largest, the most massive and capital-intensive gas project in the world', with estimated construction costs of $34 billion. The pipeline would allow Russia to partially replace lost European gas sales while giving China an alternative to Middle Eastern LNG imports.

The conflict has shifted the calculus. China imports more than half its LNG from the Middle East, and Hormuz vulnerability makes land-based supply from Russia more attractive. However, negotiations remain stuck on pricing and volume commitments — Russia wants fixed offtake guarantees; China prefers flexibility. Putin left Beijing in May without a final agreement.
sections.whyItMatters: The pipeline would reshape Eurasian gas flows but requires both sides to compromise. Russia needs Chinese demand to replace European markets; China wants price leverage and supply security. The Hormuz disruption strengthens Russia's negotiating position but doesn't resolve the fundamental disagreement over contract terms.

sections.heading: China Tightens Critical Mineral Controls with New Framework
sections.body:
China has implemented sweeping new regulations on critical mineral supply chains, with the 79-article implementation framework taking effect June 15. Premier Li Qiang promulgated rules that coordinate mining, processing, utilization, and stockpiling for rare earths and other strategic minerals, as reported by the South China Morning Post.

The framework empowers China's State Council to identify critical minerals and enforce planning controls, restrictions, and mining authorizations. Beijing has elevated resource security to 'an economic and national security level,' emphasizing internal supply, strategic management, and stockpiling.

The move comes as China's export controls on rare earths — imposed in April 2025 after Trump's Liberation Day tariffs — have become a key tool in trade and technology competition. Following Trump's recent visit to Beijing, the White House said China would 'address US concerns over supply chain shortages of rare earths and other critical minerals', though the new framework suggests Beijing intends to maintain tight control.

Separately, Forbes reports that Washington's critical minerals diplomacy has struggled to move beyond memorandums of understanding. The Lobito Corridor in Africa — designed to connect copper and cobalt production to Atlantic export markets — faces financing delays and implementation challenges. In Kazakhstan, described as potentially the 'Saudi Arabia of Critical Minerals', US engagement remains limited despite the country's sophistication and stability.
sections.whyItMatters: China continues to institutionalize its dominance over critical mineral supply chains while US efforts to build alternative networks remain largely aspirational. The gap between American announcements and deployed capital is becoming visible to partner governments and markets.

sections.heading: Uranium Supply Gains Strategic Attention as Nuclear Demand Rises
sections.body:
Urenco USA announced on June 2 that it is expanding by nearly 50% the only US facility currently enriching commercial uranium, Reuters reports. The investment is backed by signed contracts and reflects growing demand as nuclear power regains favor for data center electricity needs.

Marketplace reports that Wyoming's uranium mining industry is making a comeback, with Uranium Energy Corporation reporting earnings amid a production boost. The US is turning to nuclear power to meet growing electricity demand, with uranium as the key ingredient.

The Reuters analysis from January noted that growing nuclear generation alongside reactor construction is tightening the uranium market and setting the stage for a price rally. Aura Energy is targeting a final investment decision for its Mauritania uranium project by end of 2026 after signing an MOU with a major international nuclear utility.
sections.whyItMatters: Nuclear is re-emerging as a strategic energy priority, particularly for AI data center electricity demand. US uranium enrichment capacity remains limited, making the Urenco expansion significant for domestic supply security.

sections.heading: Turkey's Energy Hub Calculus Shifts with Regional Conflicts
sections.body:
Regional conflicts are quietly redrawing energy geography around Turkey, according to analysis from the Institude think tank. President Erdoğan highlighted Azerbaijan gas flowing into Syria since August 2025 and growing use of the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline for Kazakh crude — demonstrating Turkey's expanding role as an energy corridor.

Kazakhstan has shifted oil exports toward the BTC route as Ukrainian drone strikes target the Caspian Pipeline Consortium terminal at Novorossiysk. Volumes rose from zero in 2022 to 1.46 million tons in 2024, settling at 1.263 million tons in 2025, with plans to raise the annual ceiling to 2.2 million tons.

However, the analysis notes structural constraints. A Trans-Caspian Pipeline connecting Turkmenistan to Azerbaijan remains blocked by Russian and Iranian opposition citing environmental concerns and geopolitical interests. The 2026 Iran war has further complicated Turkish hub ambitions — Israel struck the South Pars gas field on March 18, halting production at the Asaluyeh processing hub that supplies 70 percent of Iran's gas.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's hub ambition rests on regional stability, which the current conflicts undermine. The BTC corridor demonstrates Turkey's transit value, but larger-scale projects remain blocked by geopolitical constraints that have only intensified with the Iran conflict.

bottomLine: Russia's first public admission of oil production decline marks a watershed moment — Ukrainian drone strikes are achieving measurable results against Russian refining infrastructure. The 300,000-400,000 bpd capacity loss compounds existing export pressures from Baltic and Black Sea terminal attacks. Meanwhile, Hormuz insurance costs have fundamentally reset tanker economics with premiums up 1,000%, and China continues to institutionalize critical mineral control while US alternative supply networks remain aspirational. The energy map is being reshaped by active conflict in real time.
sources: Oil Price, Financial Express, Reuters, Bloomberg, Strait Times, South China Morning Post, Forbes, Marketplace, Institude, S&P Global Energy, Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center
